key_departures.impact: Bears traded Moore and a 2026 5th-round pick to the Bills in exchange for a 2026 2nd-round pick. Moore posted a career-low 50 catches for 682 yards in 2025 as younger options Rome Odunze, Luther Burden III, and Colston Loveland ate into his targets. The trade saved approximately $16.5M in cap space. With Odunze, Burden, and Loveland all on rookie deals, this is a manageable loss, not a critical hole.

Dalman surprisingly retired at age 27, just one year after signing a landmark 3-year, $42M contract. He was a Pro Bowl center and a key anchor for Caleb Williams. His sudden retirement created an unexpected hole at center and cost the Bears ~$10M in cap savings. Immediately accelerated need for a center replacement.

Bears released Edmunds, saving $15M in cap space. He signed a 3-year, $36M deal with the Giants. He was a starter in Chicago's defense but cap casualty after Bears needed to clear space. His departure created a legitimate hole at off-ball LB alongside injured T.J. Edwards.

All-Pro safety (league-high 7 INTs in 2025) hit free agency. A Byard reunion appears close per reports but not yet confirmed. His departure from under contract creates a major hole — he was the best safety in Chicago's secondary and one of the team's defensive leaders.

Starting safety hit free agency. Reports indicate 'no shot' he returns to Chicago. Posted 93 tackles, 8 PBUs, and 1 INT in 2025. His departure alongside Byard leaving means the Bears had zero safeties under contract entering free agency — a massive positional void.
Joined Bears mid-season and became an impactful piece in Dennis Allen's defense. Social media activity suggested a split with the team before free agency. Not expected to return.

Solid developmental EDGE/rotational piece who signed a 1-year deal worth up to $4M with the Texans. His departure thins the EDGE rotation behind Montez Sweat, especially with Dayo Odeyingbo recovering from Achilles surgery.
Veteran nose tackle hit free agency and is not expected to return. Was an underrated part of Chicago's defensive front. His absence directly created the need for Gallimore.
Depth WR on a veteran-minimum deal departed. Posted 30 catches for 315 yards before role diminished. His loss is manageable given Luther Burden's emergence and the existing WR room depth.
Depth LB released to save ~$2M in cap space. Thin impact on 53-man given limited role, but further thins an already-depleted LB room.

Career-breakout year with 5 INTs, tied 2nd in NFL. Spotrac projected him at 3yr/$50M. Not expected to return at that price. His departure would be significant but the Bears have Jaylon Johnson, Kyler Gordon, and Tyrique Stevenson at CB.

signings.analysis: Chicago's first and biggest splash of free agency. Bryant, a Super Bowl champion with Seattle, started 15 regular-season games and recorded 66 tackles and 4 INTs. The 3yr/$40M deal with $25.75M guaranteed is a real investment — this is their guy at one safety spot. However, it only fills one of two starting safety vacancies. The Bears still need to determine who starts opposite Bryant — likely either a reunion with Kevin Byard (close per reports) or a draft pick. S remains a partial need.

Strong signing to replace Tremaine Edmunds. Bush re-emerged in Cleveland, posting 125 tackles, 3 INTs (2 returned for TDs), 2 sacks, and 2 forced fumbles. The 3yr/$30M with $21M guaranteed is a legitimate starter investment. He pairs with re-signed D'Marco Jackson. However, T.J. Edwards is recovering from a fractured fibula and Noah Sewell is out indefinitely with a torn Achilles — Chicago is still thin at LB depth. LB moves from a Tier 1 need to Tier 2/3 with Bush and Jackson in place, but depth remains a concern.

Signed as a nose tackle replacement for Andrew Billings. Gallimore played all 17 games for the Colts in 2025 with 3.5 sacks and 38 tackles. The 2yr/$12M deal is below starter-tier AAV ($10-18M market), landing firmly in depth territory. He is primarily a run defender who should complement Gervon Dexter and Shemar Turner. The Bears filled the immediate nose tackle void but did not make a high-upside IDL investment — IDL remains a legitimate draft need.

Re-signed on a team-friendly 2yr/$7.5M deal (base ~$4M/year per reports). Jackson earned NFC Defensive Player of the Week twice and is now in line to start opposite T.J. Edwards (when healthy) in Dennis Allen's defense. The contract structure is depth/rotational-tier money for a player being asked to start — low financial risk but signals the Bears view him as a starter by necessity, not design. Combined with Bush, the LB room is serviceable but not elite.

Acquired via trade (Bears sent 2027 5th-round pick) immediately after Drew Dalman's shocking retirement. Bradbury carries a $5.7M cap hit for 2026 — well below the starter-tier IOL market ($10-16M AAV). He is an experienced center who helped develop Drake Maye in New England, giving Ben Johnson a familiar option (Johnson knows him from the NFC North). However, this is clearly a bridge solution, not a long-term answer. The Bears likely still want to draft a center. IOL remains a legitimate tier 2-3 draft need.

Re-signed on a 1-year, $5M base ($10M max with incentives) deal. Jones is a bridge option while Ozzy Trapilo (patellar tendon tear) recovers — Trapilo is expected to miss most or all of 2026. Jones lost his job in 2025 after struggles returning from ankle surgery and was benched after Week 4. His 1-year structure screams 'prove-it/bridge deal' — the Bears have not committed to Jones long-term and are likely still looking at OT in the draft. OT remains a real need.

Re-signed as an exclusive rights free agent (ERFA) on a 1-year, $1M deal. Will compete for the LT starting job alongside Braxton Jones and Kiran Amegadjie. Logs as a depth/camp competition signing. Does not independently address the OT need.

Re-signed restricted free agent (RFA) on a 2yr/$4.97M deal with $2.45M guaranteed at signing. Hardy is primarily a special teams ace who also plays LB and DE in a versatile reserve role — he logged 725 special teams snaps over the last two seasons. His retention prevents further depth erosion at EDGE but does not address the starter-level EDGE need. EDGE remains a priority concern.

Re-signed on a 2yr/$5.5M deal (max $8M). Keenum hasn't thrown a pass in an NFL game since 2023 but is valued as a mentor and player-coach hybrid in Caleb Williams' QB room. Ben Johnson credited him with being invaluable to Williams' development. This is a non-position-need signing — QB is locked with Williams as the franchise QB and Tyson Bagent as backup.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Coby Bryant (3yr/$40M, $25.75M guaranteed) fills one starting safety spot with a real investment. However, the Bears still need a second starter opposite Bryant — Byard is reportedly close to a deal but not confirmed, Brisker is reportedly gone. With zero safeties under contract at the start of free agency, and only one spot filled, S drops from Tier 1 to Tier 2 but is not resolved. If Byard re-signs (close per reports), it moves to Tier 3.

Devin Bush (3yr/$30M, $21M guaranteed) and D'Marco Jackson (2yr/$7.5M) give the Bears two starters. However, T.J. Edwards is recovering from a fractured fibula, Noah Sewell is lost for the season with a torn Achilles, and depth is extremely thin. The Bears are set at the top but need draft-capital investment to build behind their starters. LB drops from Tier 1 to Tier 2.

Garrett Bradbury (trade, $5.7M cap hit) is a bridge center who addresses the immediate void from Drew Dalman's retirement, but at below-starter-market AAV on what is effectively a 1-year arrangement. The Bears almost certainly want a center in the 2026 draft. IOL need is unchanged in tier — still Tier 2 — but is partially mitigated as a 'starter TBD' situation.

Braxton Jones (1yr/$5M base/$10M max) re-signed as a bridge while Ozzy Trapilo recovers from a patellar tendon tear. Jones is a prove-it deal for a player who lost his starting job in 2025. The one-year structure clearly signals Chicago is not satisfied with the position long-term and will likely target OT in the draft. OT moves from Tier 1 to Tier 2 — a starter is in place but not a long-term solution.

Montez Sweat is the only true starter-quality EDGE on the roster. Dominique Robinson (rotational) departed to Houston. Dayo Odeyingbo is recovering from Achilles surgery with uncertain return timeline. Daniel Hardy's re-signing is depth/ST-only. The Bears did NOT sign a meaningful EDGE pass rusher in free agency — they missed on Maxx Crosby (traded to Baltimore) and reportedly couldn't afford Jaelan Phillips (signed with Carolina for 4yr/$120M). EDGE stays Tier 1 — this is still the most pressing unaddressed need heading into the draft.

Neville Gallimore (2yr/$12M) replaces Andrew Billings as the nose tackle. He is a run-stuffer who adds durability but his AAV ($6M) is at the floor of the starter-tier market. Gervon Dexter and Shemar Turner provide interior depth. The immediate hole is patched but the unit lacks a true disruptive interior presence. IDL remains Tier 2 — this is still a draft-worthy position.

DJ Moore (starter) was traded to Buffalo. Olamide Zaccheaus (depth) left for Atlanta. The Bears retain Rome Odunze, Luther Burden III (both on rookie deals), and have Colston Loveland at TE. However, DJ Moore's departure removes the only veteran WR1 experience from the room. With three young pass-catchers handling primary duties, the Bears escalate WR from Tier 3 to Tier 2 — not an emergency need but a legitimate draft target for a veteran receiver or developmental WR opposite Odunze.

Nahshon Wright (5 INTs, breakout 2025 season) is expected to leave in free agency, seeking 3yr/$50M+ on the open market. The Bears retain Jaylon Johnson, Kyler Gordon, and Tyrique Stevenson. Johnson is a top-tier CB1 when healthy. Losing Wright to free agency removes meaningful starting depth and a ball-hawking presence. CB moves from Tier 3 to Tier 2 — worth addressing in mid-rounds of the draft.

key_additions.headline: Super Bowl champion safety (4 INTs in 2025) fills Bears' most desperate positional void
Revitalized LB (125 tackles, 2 pick-sixes) replaces Edmunds as Chicago rebuilds its linebacker corps
Durable nose tackle (3.5 sacks, all 17 games in 2025) plugs the hole left by departing Andrew Billings

key_additions.contract_summary: Trade (2027 5th-rd pick to NE); $5.7M cap hit
key_additions.headline: Veteran center acquired via trade days after Drew Dalman's shocking retirement at age 27
Re-signed starting-caliber LB who earned NFC Defensive Player of the Week twice in 2025
Bridge LT retained on prove-it deal while Ozzy Trapilo recovers from patellar tendon surgery
Top special-teamer (725 ST snaps over 2 seasons) re-signed to versatile reserve/EDGE role
QB mentor/backup retained to continue developing Caleb Williams in Ben Johnson's system

key_losses.contract_summary: Traded to BUF for 2026 2nd-rd pick (Bears sent 5th-rd pick)
key_losses.headline: Veteran WR1 traded for 2nd-round pick in cap-saving move; Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III step into expanded roles
Pro Bowl center shockingly retired at 27, forcing Bears to scramble for a center replacement before free agency
Starting linebacker released to create $15M in cap space; Devin Bush signed as direct replacement
All-Pro safety (league-high 7 INTs in 2025) hit free agency; a reunion deal is reportedly close but not yet confirmed

key_losses.contract_summary: UFA ('no shot' of return per reports)
key_losses.headline: Starting safety departed, leaving Bears with zero returning safeties from 2025 starter pool
Breakout CB (5 INTs, 2nd in NFL) testing free market after dominant 2025 season
Rotational EDGE departs, further thinning a pass-rush group already dependent on Montez Sweat

notes: Kevin Byard deal is reportedly 'close' but unconfirmed as of March 10 — not included in signings. Jaquan Brisker and Nahshon Wright are widely reported as departures but have not yet signed elsewhere as of this date. All contract figures sourced from ESPN (Courtney Cronin), Adam Schefter (ESPN), Tom Pelissero (NFL Network), Jeremy Fowler (ESPN), Over The Cap, and Spotrac. Theo Benedet contract confirmed via OverTheCap. Coby Bryant guaranteed ($25.75M) confirmed via Tom Pelissero/NFL Network.
